<!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kotohira Shrines]] [[simple:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Kotohira shrines]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kotohira Shrines]] [[simple:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Kotohira shrines]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kotohira Shrines]] [[simple:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Kotohira shrines]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}
== Konpira Shrines, Kotohira Shrines, and Konpira Shrines across Japan == === Konpira === ==== Hokkaido ==== * {{ill|Nemuro Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅神社 (根室市)|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nemuro Kotohira Shrine|qq=}} ([[:en:Nemuro, Hokkaido|Nemuro]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]]) – Founded in 1806 * Hirotomi Konpira Shrine ({{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka Town|qq=|1=Hidakachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q854578}}) * Konpira Shrine ([[:en:Makubetsu, Hokkaido|Makubetsu Town]])

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. <!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kotohira Shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Kotohira shrines]] {{wikidata link|Q11646488}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kotohira Shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Kotohira shrines]] {{wikidata link|Q11646488}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

revision.comment: Bot: Add Wikidata no short description - based on Wikidata English short description
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}
== Konpira Shrines, Kotohira Shrines, and Konpira Shrines across Japan == === Konpira === ==== Hokkaido ==== * {{ill|Nemuro Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅神社 (根室市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nemuro Kotohira Shrine|qq=}} ([[:en:Nemuro, Hokkaido|Nemuro]], [[:en:Hokkaido|Hokkaido]]) – Founded in 1806 * Hirotomi Konpira Shrine ({{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka Town|1=Hidakachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q854578}}) * Konpira Shrine ([[:en:Makubetsu, Hokkaido|Makubetsu Town]])

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--><!--Kotohira Shrine--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> [[en:Kotohira Shrines]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[en:Kotohira shrines]] {{wikidata link|Q11646488}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

[[en:Kotohira shrines]] {{wikidata link|Q11646488}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity [[:en:Ōmononushi|Ōmononushi]] ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in [[:en:Kotohira, Kagawa|Kotohira]], [[:en:Nakatado District, Kagawa|Nakatado District]], [[:en:Kagawa Prefecture|Kagawa Prefecture]]. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

== Overview == During the [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]], the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the [[:en:Shinbutsu bunri|Shinbutsu bunri]] (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the [[:en:Haibutsu kishaku|Haibutsu kishaku]] (abolition of Buddhism) during the [[:en:Meiji Restoration|Meiji Restoration]], the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, [[:en:Kotohira-gū|Kotohiragū]], worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still [[:en:Konpira Gongen|Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen]] that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11646488]] Wikidata name: Kotohira shrine [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:40, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]]
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]]
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]]
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]]
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|1=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District|1=Nakatado District, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū|1=Kotohira-gū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen|1=Konpira Gongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

[[en:Kotohira shrines]] {{wikidata link|Q11646488}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]]
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|1=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District|1=Nakatado District, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū|1=Kotohira-gū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen|1=Konpira Gongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

[[en:Kotohira shrines]] {{wikidata link|Q11646488}} [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:translated pages with valid en interwikis]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[en:Kotohira Shrines]][[ja:金刀比羅神社]][[simple:Kotohira shrines]]
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|1=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District|1=Nakatado District, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū|1=Kotohira-gū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen|1=Konpira Gongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|WD=Q10936701|WD=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|1=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|WD=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|WD=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District|1=Nakatado District, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|WD=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|WD=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|WD=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|WD=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|WD=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū|1=Kotohira-gū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|WD=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen|1=Konpira Gongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}

revision.comment: Bot: strip HTML comments; consolidate 14 interlanguage link(s) into wikidata link template (has duplicate-language pairs) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/25098527498|time triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
'''Konpira Shrine''' (金刀比羅神社), also known as '''Kotohira Shrine''' (琴平神社), '''Koto Hira Shrine''' (事比羅神社), or '''Konpira Shrine''' (金比羅神社), is a Shinto shrine dedicated to the deity {{ill|Ōmononushi|en|Ōmononushi|qid=Q10936701|qid=Q10936701|lt=Ōmononushi|1=Ōmononushi}} ([[Konpira Gongen]]) with its head shrine located in {{ill|Kotohira, Kagawa|en|Kotohira, Kagawa|qid=Q1271483|lt=Kotohira|1=Kotohira, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Nakatado District, Kagawa|en|Nakatado District, Kagawa|qid=Q1206155|lt=Nakatado District|1=Nakatado District, Kagawa}}, {{ill|Kagawa Prefecture|en|Kagawa Prefecture|qid=Q161454|lt=Kagawa Prefecture|1=Kagawa Prefecture}}. It is a place of worship for the Konpira faith and has many branches across Japan. Some branches use the term "shrine" (神社), while others use "palace" (宮).
== Overview == During the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}}, the Konpira faith spread widely across Japan through the efforts of maritime traders and merchants as shipborne commerce became more common. Branch shrines were established in many locations. Following the {{ill|Shinbutsu bunri|en|Shinbutsu bunri|qid=Q1970535|lt=Shinbutsu bunri|1=Shinbutsu bunri}} (separation of Shinto and Buddhism) and the {{ill|Haibutsu kishaku|en|Haibutsu kishaku|qid=Q702298|lt=Haibutsu kishaku|1=Haibutsu kishaku}} (abolition of Buddhism) during the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|en|Meiji Restoration|qid=Q8707|lt=Meiji Restoration|1=Meiji Restoration}}, the syncretic Konpira Daigongen faith was dismantled. Konpira shrines became Shinto shrines dedicated to Ōmononushi no Kami as their primary deity. There are about 600 shrines nationwide that align with the head shrine, {{ill|Kotohira-gū|en|Kotohira-gū|qid=Q94760|lt=Kotohiragū|1=Kotohira-gū}}, worshipping Ōmononushi no Kami. Notably, there are still {{ill|Konpira Gongen|en|Konpira Gongen|qid=Q167077|lt=Shrines venerating Konpira Daigongen|1=Konpira Gongen}} that continue to enshrine Konpira Daigongen without changing the main deity. {{main|Kotohiragū|Konpira Gongen}}
== Konpira Shrines, Kotohira Shrines, and Konpira Shrines across Japan == === Konpira === ==== Hokkaido ==== * {{ill|Nemuro Kotohira Shrine|ja|金刀比羅神社 (根室市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nemuro Kotohira Shrine|qq=}} ({{ill|Nemuro, Hokkaido|en|Nemuro, Hokkaido|qid=Q502205|lt=Nemuro|1=Nemuro, Hokkaido}}, {{ill|Hokkaido|en|Hokkaido|qid=Q35581|lt=Hokkaido|1=Hokkaido}}) – Founded in 1806 * Hirotomi Konpira Shrine ({{ill|Hidaka Town|ja|日高町|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidaka Town|1=Hidakachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q854578}}) * Konpira Shrine ({{ill|Makubetsu, Hokkaido|en|Makubetsu, Hokkaido|qid=Q1210509|lt=Makubetsu Town|1=Makubetsu, Hokkaido}})
